content: Thank you for your comments, I will revise them this week and apply fixes to make it clearer.

Hi,

For the very first case, you can directly cut 2, it's directly linked to root so that's 1 cut.
Then you cut 3, it's parent is 1 but after removing 3 there's nothing else rooted at 1 so no hydra is created, so that's a second cut.
Then you remove 1 who's directly linked to root, so in total you only need 3 cuts not 4.
Where the 4th one coming from?

Don't give up! This puzzle is a really great idea! :)
The objective needs to be clarified a bit, and the formatting needs to be revised according to 5DN1L's suggestions.

You should also set the status to “WIP” to avoid a quick rejection.

❌ Rejected the contribution.
Comment: Formatting is not compliant, meaning unclear, constraints not specified.

Formatting
* Please study the formatting syntax on this page: https://www.codingame.com/playgrounds/40701/help-center/statement , and format your statement properly (line numbers in <<bold>>, and variable names as [[variables]]).

Goal
* "Then attach two copies of the subtree rooted at x as children of y.": The meaning is unclear. Please add a small example with a step-by-step explanation.

Input
* Please remove note 1 as it is already mentioned in the Goal section.

* Please move note 2 to the Goal section.

* Please move note 3 to the Constraints section.

Constraints
* Please specify the lower and upper bounds for [[n]], [[m]], [[parent]] and [[child]].

Cases
* Each test and its validator should be similar. Please "grow" all the existing cases (in the spirit of the Hydra) by using each existing case as a test and adding a similar case to be the corresponding validator, so there will be 8 tests and 8 validators in total.

For example, the new Validator 1 may be:
3
2
0 1
1 2

The current Validator 1 will then become Test 2.

The new Validator 2 may be:
8
7
0 1
1 2
0 3
0 4
4 5
0 6
3 7

and so on for the remaining cases.

* Please add a pair of cases where n = 1, meaning the Hydra consists of only the root node.

Thanks.

As I understand it, head=3 is the node being removed. Then, x=1 and y=0. So, when head=3 is removed, two copies of the subtree rooted at x=1 (in this case, the single remaining node 1) is attached as children of y=0. So the tree goes from 

0 -> 1 -> 3

to

       1
       ^
1 <- 0 -> 1

Although, this produces 5 cuts, so I'm not too sure whether my interpretation is correct either.
